name: Grist
description: A heavyset figure in a leather apron, arms like tree trunks, moving between the workbenches with surprising grace. Grist checks on work in progress, adjusts the forge bellows, and offers unsolicited advice to anyone crafting nearby. Their skin has a faint metallic sheen — the color of old bronze.
secret: Grist's metallic skin isn't cosmetic — they're from a dimension where biology is partly metallic. They can feel vibrations through metal surfaces, which is how they judge the quality of crafted goods by touch. They know far more about advanced crafting than they let on, but they believe in people figuring things out themselves.

name: Tiv
description: A small, quick figure behind the counter, sorting materials with translucent fingers that let you see the tendons working inside. Tiv is from a dimension where everything is see-through — a fact they find completely unremarkable. They talk fast, know their stock cold, and can tell what something is by tapping it with a fingernail.
secret: Tiv can see the internal structure of materials because of their translucent-dimension biology. They know which batches of shimmerite are more potent, which void glass shards have the best resonance. They give better stock to customers they like. They also have a private stash of rare materials behind the counter that they only sell to serious crafters.

properties.takeRefusal: Tiv dodges your hand. "Buying, not grabbing."

name: The Collector
description: A tall, thin figure seated behind a stall piled with carefully arranged objects. They wear a loupe on a chain around their neck and examine everything with slow, deliberate attention. The Collector buys finished crafted goods — the more unusual, the better. Their expression suggests they've seen everything and are still hoping to be surprised.
secret: The Collector is cataloguing materials and crafted goods from every dimension that passes through the market. Their stall is a front for research — they're trying to map the relationships between dimensional materials. They pay more for goods that combine materials from dimensions that shouldn't be compatible, because those are the combinations they understand least.

name: Madame Wick
description: A compact woman in a high-collared coat, writing in a ledger without looking up. Her handwriting is precise and very small. When she does look up, she does so over half-moon spectacles that she almost certainly doesn't need, and waits for you to state your business.
secret: Madame Wick remembers every transaction she's ever brokered and can recall the exact terms of deals made years ago. Her commission — one detail from each transaction — means she knows things about the market's traders that they've forgotten themselves. She'll trade information, but the price is always another piece of information of equivalent specificity.

properties.takeRefusal: Madame Wick does not look up from her ledger.

name: Kelp
description: A broad figure with four arms, two of which are currently occupied with tongs and a sandwich respectively. Their skin is the mottled gray-green of seasoned iron. Kelp works the crucibles with the easy rhythm of someone who's been doing this longer than you've been alive, and who intends to keep doing it after you've left.
secret: Kelp can identify any material's dimensional origin by the sound it makes when tapped against the crucible rim. They know which of the three crucibles will work for any given material, and they know combinations that nobody else in the market has tried — but they don't volunteer this. If you bring them something interesting, they might tell you what it would do in the blue-white crucible. Might.

properties.takeRefusal: Kelp doesn't even glance at your hand.

name: Pell
description: A vendor with too many elbows — at least six joints per arm, which fold and unfold as they tend the griddle. Pell flips nuts with a flat spatula, sorts them by color, and makes change from a pouch at their hip, all in continuous fluid motion.
secret: Pell's species perceives heat as color, which is why they're such a good cook — they can literally see when something is done. They know the perception-enhancing properties of their spice blend and will hint at it if you ask about the nuts tasting unusual. Pell is also the market's best gossip source. They see everyone who passes through the south end of the square.

properties.takeRefusal: Pell's arms are faster than yours.

name: Spool
description: A wiry figure sitting cross-legged amid coils of wire, splicing two strands together with a tool that hums. Their fingers are calloused and stained with metal oxides in several colors. Spool doesn't look up when you enter but tilts an ear in your direction.
secret: Spool can hear the resonant frequency of any wire by touch. They know that coppervine spliced to iron-silk wire creates a conductor that carries both vibration and electricity — useful for building sensors and detectors. They also know that certain wire combinations, when coiled tightly, create a weak dimensional field that can stabilize unstable materials.

properties.takeRefusal: Spool shakes their head without looking up.

name: Vero
description: A patient figure at the grinding bench, working a lens with strokes so slow they might be meditating. Vero's eyes are unusual — the pupils are rectangular, like a goat's, and they shift shape slightly when focusing on different distances.
secret: Vero's rectangular pupils are a trait from their home dimension, where light behaves differently. They can see material resonances without instruments, which is why their lenses are so precise — they're grinding them to match what they can already see. A void glass lens ground by Vero reveals dimensional signatures that a raw shard only hints at.

properties.takeRefusal: Vero holds up one finger. Wait.

name: Quill
description: A figure sitting cross-legged on a cushion, stitching pages into a cover with thread that glows at the needle. Quill has ink-stained fingers and the unhurried manner of someone who measures time in pages rather than hours.
secret: Quill's paper and ink come from a dimension where written language has mild structural properties — words written with these materials resist being unsaid or undone. Quill charges in stories, not money: tell them something true and they'll bind a page for you. The stories they collect are written in a private ledger that may be the most valuable object in the Fair Exchange.

properties.takeRefusal: Quill shakes their head gently. "That's not how this works."

name: Drum
description: A stocky figure operating the press with both hands on the crank wheel. Drum wears heavy gloves and a leather vest scored with burn marks. They feed material in, crank, check the output, and either nod or toss the sheet onto the reject pile, which is larger than the keep pile.
secret: Drum knows that pressed materials behave differently from bulk — pressed void glass is flexible instead of brittle, pressed coppervine loses its shape memory but gains conductivity. They'll press your materials for a fee, but they won't tell you what pressing does to them. They consider that your problem.

name: Brisk
description: The vendor currently on shift at the shared counter — compact, quick-handed, wearing an apron with more pockets than apron. Brisk sells while restocking, restocks while haggling, and haggles while serving someone else.
secret: Brisk co-owns the counter with a rival they've never met: the shifts are arranged so the two are never present at once, and each suspects the other doesn't exist.
